# 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 📌 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Gray code. List any four of its properties.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Gray code is a non-weighted binary code in which successive code words differ by **only one bit**.

Properties (any four, ½ mark each + ½ mark for definition = 3 marks):
1. **Cyclic** — last and first codes differ by one bit
2. **Unit-distance** — successive codes differ in exactly one bit
3. **Unweighted** — bits carry no positional weight
4. **Reflective** — generated by mirror-and-prefix method
5. **Non-self-complementing** — complement of Gray ≠ Gray of complement

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]**
**Differentiate between even parity and odd parity with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Aspect | Even Parity | Odd Parity |
|--------|-------------|------------|
| Rule | Total 1's (data + P) is even | Total 1's (data + P) is odd |
| P for data `1010` (2 ones) | 0 | 1 |
| P formula | $P = \bigoplus D_i$ | $P = \overline{\bigoplus D_i}$ |

(1 mark for definition each + 1 mark for example comparison)

---

## 📌 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice — 14 Marks Each)

---

### **📘 QUESTION A (14 MARKS) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**A(a)** Explain Gray code. Convert the binary number **$101101_2$** to Gray code and verify by converting back. **(7 marks)**

**A(b)** Define parity. With a neat block diagram and truth table, explain the design of a 3-bit **even parity generator** using XOR gates. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question A(a) [7 marks]

**Definition (1 mark):**
Gray code is a unit-distance, cyclic, unweighted binary code where successive codes differ by exactly one bit. It eliminates race-around conditions in encoders and is used in K-maps.

**Conversion Rule (1 mark):**
- MSB preserved: $G_{n-1} = B_{n-1}$
- $G_i = B_{i+1} \oplus B_i$

**Binary → Gray for $101101_2$ (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_5 &= B_5 = 1 \\
G_4 &= B_5 \oplus B_4 = 1 \oplus 0 = 1 \\
G_3 &= B_4 \oplus B_3 = 0 \oplus 1 = 1 \\
G_2 &= B_3 \oplus B_2 = 1 \oplus 1 = 0 \\
G_1 &= B_2 \oplus B_1 = 1 \oplus 0 = 1 \\
G_0 &= B_1 \oplus B_0 = 0 \oplus 1 = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

∴ **Gray code = 111011**

**Gray → Binary verification (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_5 &= G_5 = 1 \\
B_4 &= B_5 \oplus G_4 = 1 \oplus 1 = 0 \\
B_3 &= B_4 \oplus G_3 = 0 \oplus 1 = 1 \\
B_2 &= B_3 \oplus G_2 = 1 \oplus 0 = 1 \\
B_1 &= B_2 \oplus G_1 = 1 \oplus 1 = 0 \\
B_0 &= B_1 \oplus G_0 = 0 \oplus 1 = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

∴ **Recovered Binary = 101101** ✓ (matches the original)

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question A(b) [7 marks]

**Definition (1 mark):**
Parity is a single-bit error-detection technique that appends one extra bit to the data word so that the total number of 1's conforms to a chosen rule (even/odd).

**Block Diagram Description (2 marks):**

For a 3-bit data $D_2 D_1 D_0$:

$$
P = D_2 \oplus D_1 \oplus D_0
$$

The parity generator uses **two XOR gates** in cascade:

- **Gate 1:** $X_1 = D_2 \oplus D_1$
- **Gate 2:** $P = X_1 \oplus D_0$

**Truth Table (3 marks):**

| $D_2$ | $D_1$ | $D_0$ | Number of 1's | Parity $P$ (Even) | Final Code |
|-------|-------|-------|---------------|-------------------|------------|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 (even) | 0 | 0000 |
| 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 (odd)  | 1 | 0011 |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 (odd)  | 1 | 0101 |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | 2 (even) | 0 | 0110 |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 (odd)  | 1 | 1001 |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | 2 (even) | 0 | 1010 |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | 2 (even) | 0 | 1100 |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 3 (odd)  | 1 | 1111 |

**Verification (1 mark):** Every row of the final code (data + P) has an even number of 1's, confirming correct even-parity generation.

---

### **📘 QUESTION B (14 MARKS) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**B(a)** Explain the concept of **logic families**. Compare **TTL, CMOS, and ECL** with respect to propagation delay, power dissipation, fan-out, and noise margin. **(7 marks)**

**B(b)** Define **universal gates**. Show how AND, OR, and NOT gates can be realised using only **NAND gates**, with Boolean expressions and logic diagrams. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question B(a) [7 marks]

**Definition of Logic Family (2 marks):**
A logic family is a set of digital ICs fabricated using the same underlying technology, sharing common electrical characteristics like propagation delay, power dissipation, fan-out, and noise margin. They share compatible logic levels allowing interoperability.

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | TTL | CMOS | ECL |
|-----------|-----|------|-----|
| **Basic Device** | BJT | MOSFET | BJT (differential) |
| **Propagation Delay** | ~10 ns | ~25 ns (older) | ~1–2 ns (fastest) |
| **Power Dissipation** | ~10 mW/gate | ~10 µW static | ~50 mW/gate (high) |
| **Fan-out** | 10 | 50+ | 25 |
| **Noise Margin** | ~0.4 V | ~1.5 V | ~0.2 V (lowest) |
| **Supply Voltage** | +5 V | +3 V to +15 V | −5.2 V |

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
- **TTL** — balanced speed & power for general-purpose 74-series designs.
- **CMOS** — best for low-power, high-density VLSI.
- **ECL** — chosen when ultra-high speed is paramount, despite high power.

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question B(b) [7 marks]

**Definition (1 mark):**
A universal gate is one which can implement any Boolean function without needing any other gate type. **NAND** and **NOR** are the two universal gates.

**Implementation of NOT using NAND (1 mark):**

$$
Y = \text{NAND}(A, A) = \overline{A \cdot A} = \overline{A}
$$

**Implementation of AND using NAND (2 marks):**

$$
Y = \text{NAND}(\text{NAND}(A, B), \text{NAND}(A, B)) = \overline{\overline{A \cdot B}} = A \cdot B
$$

**Implementation of OR using NAND (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y &= \text{NAND}(\overline{A}, \overline{B}) \\
  &= \text{NAND}(\text{NAND}(A, A), \text{NAND}(B, B)) \\
  &= \overline{\overline{A} \cdot \overline{B}} \\
  &= A + B \quad \text{(by De Morgan's theorem)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic Diagrams / Conclusion (1 mark):**
Since NOT, AND, OR are all realisable using only NAND gates, **NAND is a universal gate**. By similar reasoning, NOR is also universal — proving that entire digital systems can be built using a single gate type, which is the foundation of IC design (e.g., 74xx NAND-only chips).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION PITFALLS — Common Mark Deductions**
>
> 1. **Gray code conversions:** Students often apply the **wrong XOR direction**. Remember: in Binary→Gray, you XOR **adjacent BINARY bits**, not Gray bits. In Gray→Binary, you XOR the **recovered binary** with the **current Gray** bit.
> 2. **Parity bit formula:** Mixing up $P = \bigoplus D_i$ (even) with $\overline{\bigoplus D_i}$ (odd). One careless bit error = full mark loss in sub-part.
> 3. **Universal gate proof:** Writing expressions without stating **De Morgan's theorem** explicitly loses 1 mark. Examiners expect **both algebraic derivation AND a labelled logic diagram**.
> 4. **Logic family comparison:** Listing parameters without numerical values (e.g., "low", "high") will attract **partial marking only**. KTU requires **quantitative** comparison.
> 5. **Truth tables:** Forgetting the **parity bit column** in the final transmitted code is a frequent oversight; show the complete transmitted 4-bit word including P.

---

## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> **Print this section before every KTU exam!**

- ✅ **Gray Code** = unit-distance, cyclic, unweighted, non-self-complementing.
- ✅ **Binary → Gray:** MSB preserved; subsequent $G_i = B_{i+1} \oplus B_i$.
- ✅ **Gray → Binary:** MSB preserved; subsequent $B_i = B_{i+1} \oplus G_i$ (recursive).
- ✅ **Gray code applications:** shaft encoders, K-maps, ADCs, asynchronous FIFOs.
- ✅ **Parity** is a **single-bit error-detection** scheme (cannot correct, detects only odd-bit errors).
- ✅ **Even parity bit:** $P = \bigoplus D_i$ ; **Odd parity bit:** $P = \overline{\bigoplus D_i}$.
- ✅ **Parity checker** output $E=1$ ⇒ error detected.
- ✅ **Basic gates:** AND ($A \cdot B$), OR ($A + B$), NOT ($\overline{A}$).
- ✅ **Universal gates:** NAND ($\overline{A \cdot B}$), NOR ($\overline{A + B}$) — can build any Boolean function.
- ✅ **NOT via NAND** = NAND(A,A); **NOT via NOR** = NOR(A,A).
- ✅ **AND via NAND** = NAND(NAND(A,B), NAND(A,B)) — two gates.
- ✅ **OR via NAND** = NAND(NAND(A,A), NAND(B,B)) — three gates.
- ✅ **Logic families:** TTL (bipolar, 5 V), CMOS (MOS, low power), ECL (bipolar, fastest).
- ✅ **Power-Delay Product (PDP)** is the **figure-of-merit** for comparing logic families.
- ✅ **Fan-out** = maximum number of similar loads a gate can drive without degradation.
- ✅ **Noise margin** quantifies immunity against voltage noise.
- ✅ **De Morgan's theorems** are the **backbone** of universal-gate derivations — must be memorised verbatim.
- ✅ **KTU mantra:** Always present **truth table + algebraic expression + logic diagram + application** for full marks.

---

> 🏁 **End of Module 1 Premium Notes — Gray Code, Parity, Logic Families, Basic & Universal Gates**

topics.title: Realisation of Logic Gates Using Transistors and Diodes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBICT304 - DESIGN OF LOGIC CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Realisation of Logic Gates Using Transistors and Diodes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Logic Gate Realisation** is the physical implementation of Boolean logic functions (AND, OR, NOT, NAND, NOR, XOR, XNOR) using discrete semiconductor devices — primarily **diodes** and **bipolar junction transistors (BJTs)** — interconnected with passive components such as **resistors** and a DC power supply ($V_{CC}$). This forms the foundation of all digital integrated circuit families including **Diode Logic (DL)**, **Resistor-Transistor Logic (RTL)**, **Diode-Transistor Logic (DTL)**, **Transistor-Transistor Logic (TTL)**, and **Emitter-Coupled Logic (ECL)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus (Module 1):** Students must understand how a **transistor operates as a switch** (Cut-off vs Saturation) and how **diodes act as unilateral voltage decision elements**. These two primitives combine to form every digital gate ever manufactured.

### 1.2 The Two Fundamental Building Blocks

| Device | Boolean Symbol | Physical Behaviour | Logic Role |
|--------|---------------|-------------------|------------|
| **Diode** | One-way valve for current | Conducts only when forward-biased ($V_{AK} \geq 0.7\,V$ for Si) | Implements **AND / OR** by voltage selection |
| **BJT (NPN)** | Voltage-controlled switch | ON (Saturation) when $V_{BE} \geq 0.7\,V$ and base current sufficient | Implements **NOT / NAND / NOR** by inversion |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Water-Pipe Analogy (Diodes as Logic Elements):**
> Imagine two water pipes (inputs A and B) joined at a junction connected to a single outlet (output Y). If a **diode** is placed on each input pipe pointing *toward* the junction, only the **lowest voltage** input can "suck" current back through its diode, leaving the output pulled to that lowest value. This is the **AND gate** in disguise — the output is LOW only if any input is LOW.
>
> Reverse the diodes (pointing *away* from junction) and the **highest voltage** wins — the **OR gate**. The diode is simply a "voltage referee" that decides who controls the output node.

For the **transistor**, think of it as a **light switch controlled by a hand-pressure sensor**:
- **No pressure on the base (input LOW)** → Switch OPEN → Output pulled HIGH through resistor
- **Sufficient pressure (input HIGH, $I_B > I_{B(min)}$)** → Switch CLOSED → Output pulled LOW

This simple mechanical picture maps directly to the **NOT gate (inverter)**.

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Engineering
- Every commercial IC (74-series TTL, 4000-series CMOS, modern FinFET CPUs) is a **direct descendant** of these primitive diode-transistor circuits.
- Understanding gate realisation explains **propagation delay, fan-out, noise margin, and power dissipation** — the four pillars of digital design.
- Foundation for **VLSI design, FPGA architecture, and ASIC development**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Transistor as a Switch — Two Operating Regions

A BJT in digital circuits operates in only **two stable states**:

**State 1 — CUT-OFF (Switch OPEN, Logic 0 at Input):**

$$V_{BE} < 0.7\,V \implies I_B \approx 0 \implies I_C \approx 0 \implies V_{CE} \approx V_{CC}$$

> The transistor is effectively an **open circuit** between Collector and Emitter. Output node is pulled HIGH to $V_{CC}$ through the collector resistor $R_C$.

**State 2 — SATURATION (Switch CLOSED, Logic 1 at Input):**

$$V_{BE} \geq 0.7\,V, \quad I_B \geq I_{B(sat)} = \frac{I_{C(sat)}}{\beta_{FC}}$$

$$I_{C(sat)} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{CE(sat)}}{R_C} \approx \frac{V_{CC}}{R_C}$$

$$V_{CE} = V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.2\,V \quad (\text{Logic 0 at output})$$

> The transistor behaves as a **closed switch** with a small saturation voltage drop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Difference between Cut-off and Saturation:**
> - **Cut-off:** Base-Emitter junction is **reverse-biased** (no injection).
> - **Saturation:** Both Base-Emitter (forward) and Base-Collector (forward) junctions are forward-biased. The transistor is "flooded" with carriers — this is what causes **storage time delay** when switching OFF.

### 2.2 Diode Logic (DL) — AND and OR Gates

#### 2.2.1 Diode AND Gate (Positive Logic)

In **positive logic**, HIGH = 1, LOW = 0.

**Circuit Operation:**
- Diodes $D_A$, $D_B$ have anodes at inputs A, B and cathodes joined at output Y.
- $R$ pulls Y up to $V_{CC}$ (typically $+5\,V$).
- **If ANY input is LOW ($0\,V$):** The corresponding diode is forward-biased (anode at $V_{CC}$, cathode at $0\,V$); Y is clamped to $\approx 0.7\,V$ (LOW).
- **If ALL inputs are HIGH ($+5\,V$):** All diodes are reverse-biased (or zero-biased); Y floats up to $V_{CC}$ (HIGH).

$$Y = A \cdot B \quad (\text{AND operation})$$

#### 2.2.2 Diode OR Gate (Positive Logic)

- Diodes have **cathodes at inputs, anodes joined at output**, and $R$ pulls Y down to **GND ($0\,V$)**.
- **If ANY input is HIGH:** That diode conducts; Y rises to $\approx V_{in} - 0.7\,V$ (HIGH).
- **If ALL inputs are LOW:** No diode conducts; Y = $0\,V$ (LOW).

$$Y = A + B \quad (\text{OR operation})$$

### 2.3 Resistor-Transistor Logic (RTL) — The NOR Gate

RTL was the **first commercially successful logic family** (introduced 1961, Fairchild).

**Circuit:** Multiple input transistors in parallel, each with a base resistor $R_B$ and a shared collector resistor $R_C$.

**Operation:**
- If **any** input is HIGH, the corresponding transistor saturates → output Y pulled LOW.
- If **all** inputs are LOW, all transistors cut off → output Y pulled HIGH through $R_C$.

$$Y = \overline{A + B} \quad (\text{NOR operation})$$

### 2.4 Diode-Transistor Logic (DTL) — The NAND Gate

DTL is **the most important gate from a Module-1 perspective** for KTU because it is the direct precursor to TTL.

**Circuit Structure:**
1. **Input stage:** Diode AND network (diodes $D_A$, $D_B$ with pull-up $R_1$).
2. **Level-shift stage:** Two series diodes ($D_1$, $D_2$) — each drops $0.7\,V$, total $1.4\,V$.
3. **Output stage:** NPN transistor with base resistor $R_B$.

**Why the two series diodes?**
- Transistor needs $V_{BE} = 0.7\,V$ to turn ON.
- AND gate output is either $\approx 0\,V$ (LOW) or $\approx 5\,V$ (HIGH).
- With two series diodes, current to base only flows when Y_diodes $> 0.7 + 0.7 + 0.7 = 2.1\,V$, providing **excellent noise immunity**.

$$Y = \overline{A \cdot B} \quad (\text{NAND operation})$$

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Typical Value | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Saturation Collector Current** | $I_{C(sat)} = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{CE(sat)}}{R_C}$ | $\approx \dfrac{V_{CC}}{R_C}$ | Max current when transistor ON |
| 2 | **Base Current at Edge of Saturation** | $I_{B(sat)} = \dfrac{I_{C(sat)}}{\beta_{FC}}$ | $\approx \dfrac{V_{CC}}{\beta_{FC} R_C}$ | Minimum $I_B$ to saturate transistor |
| 3 | **Overdrive Factor (Forced $\beta$)** | $\beta_{forced} = \dfrac{I_{C(sat)}}{I_B}$ | $\beta_{forced} \ll \beta_{FC}$ | Ensures hard saturation |
| 4 | **Logic HIGH Output (RTL/DTL)** | $V_{OH} = V_{CC} - I_{C(leak)} R_C$ | $\approx V_{CC}$ | Output when all transistors OFF |
| 5 | **Logic LOW Output** | $V_{OL} = V_{CE(sat)}$ | $\approx 0.2\,V$ | Output when transistor saturated |
| 6 | **Diode Forward Drop (Silicon)** | $V_F$ | $0.7\,V$ | Voltage across conducting Si diode |
| 7 | **Diode Forward Drop (Germanium)** | $V_F$ | $0.3\,V$ | Voltage across conducting Ge diode |
| 8 | **Noise Margin LOW** | $NM_L = V_{IL(max)} - V_{OL(max)}$ | $\approx 0.4\,V$ | Allowed noise on LOW signal |
| 9 | **Noise Margin HIGH** | $NM_H = V_{OH(min)} - V_{IH(min)}$ | $\approx 0.4\,V$ | Allowed noise on HIGH signal |
| 10 | **Fan-out (BJT)** | $N = \dfrac{I_{OH}}{I_{IH}}$ | $\geq 10$ | Max gate inputs that can be driven |
| 11 | **Power Dissipation (Static)** | $P_D = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CC(avg)}$ | mW range | Heat generated per gate |
| 12 | **Propagation Delay** | $t_{pd} = \dfrac{t_{pLH} + t_{pHL}}{2}$ | ns range | Speed of logic transition |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** The noise margin equations ($NM_L$, $NM_H$) are exactly what circuit designers use to decide whether a logic family is suitable for **automotive (high-noise)**, **medical (low-noise precision)**, or **aerospace (radiation-hardened)** applications. DTL's two-diode level shift gave it $\approx 1.4\,V$ noise margin — far better than RTL's $\approx 0.2\,V$.

### 2.6 Comparison of Early Logic Families

| Family | Full Form | Gate Realised | Noise Margin | Speed | Year |
|--------|-----------|---------------|--------------|-------|------|
| **DL** | Diode Logic | AND, OR only | Poor (no inversion) | Fast | 1950s |
| **RTL** | Resistor-Transistor Logic | NOR | $\approx 0.2\,V$ | Slow | 1961 |
| **DTL** | Diode-Transistor Logic | NAND | $\approx 1.4\,V$ | Medium | 1962 |
| **TTL** | Transistor-Transistor Logic | NAND | $\approx 0.4\,V$ | Fast | 1963 |
| **ECL** | Emitter-Coupled Logic | OR/NOR | $\approx 0.2\,V$ | Very Fast | 1962 |
| **CMOS** | Complementary MOS | NAND/NOR/Inverter | $\approx 1.5\,V$ | Medium | 1968 |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Analysis: The DTL NAND Gate

#### 3.1.1 Circuit Description

Given a DTL NAND gate with:
- $V_{CC} = +5\,V$
- $R_1 = 5\,k\Omega$ (input pull-up)
- $D_A, D_B$ = input diodes (anodes at A, B; cathodes at node P)
- $D_1, D_2$ = level-shift diodes (anode at P, cathode at base of $Q_1$)
- $R_2 = 2\,k\Omega$ (base resistor of $Q_1$)
- $Q_1$ = NPN silicon BJT ($\beta_{FC} = 50$, $V_{BE(on)} = 0.7\,V$, $V_{CE(sat)} = 0.2\,V$)
- $R_C = 2\,k\Omega$ (collector pull-up)
- Node labels: **P** = AND-stage output, **B** = transistor base, **Y** = final output

**Task:** Derive the output Y for all four input combinations.

---

#### 3.1.2 Case 1: A = 0 (0 V), B = 0 (0 V)

**Step 1 — Determine which input diode conducts:**

Since both A and B are at $0\,V$, both diodes $D_A$ and $D_B$ have their cathodes at $0\,V$ and anodes connected to the same node P (which is pulled up toward $V_{CC}$ via $R_1$). Both are forward-biased.

**Step 2 — Calculate voltage at node P:**

The conducting diodes clamp P to one diode drop above the LOW input:

$$V_P = V_A + V_{F(D_A)} = 0 + 0.7 = 0.7\,V$$

**Step 3 — Check if level-shift diodes $D_1, D_2$ conduct:**

For $D_1$ to conduct, the voltage at the cathode of $D_1$ (which is the base B) must be at least $V_P - V_{F(D_1)} = 0.7 - 0.7 = 0\,V$. But even if the base is at $0\,V$, the chain requires:

$$V_B = V_P - V_{F(D_1)} - V_{F(D_2)} = 0.7 - 0.7 - 0.7 = -0.7\,V$$

This is impossible (base cannot be negative with passive pull-down). Therefore **$D_1$ and $D_2$ are OFF** and **$Q_1$ is OFF**.

**Step 4 — Determine output Y:**

With $Q_1$ in cut-off, no collector current flows. Output Y is pulled up to $V_{CC}$:

$$Y = V_{CC} = +5\,V \quad (\text{Logic HIGH} = 1)$$

**Result:** $Y = \overline{0 \cdot 0} = \overline{0} = 1$ ✓

---

#### 3.1.3 Case 2: A = 0 (0 V), B = 1 (5 V)

**Step 1 — Identify conducting diode:**

Diode $D_A$ has anode at P ($>0\,V$ via $R_1$) and cathode at $0\,V$ → **forward-biased and conducts**.
Diode $D_B$ has cathode at P and anode at $5\,V$ → **reverse-biased and OFF**.

**Step 2 — Voltage at P:**

$$V_P = V_A + V_{F(D_A)} = 0 + 0.7 = 0.7\,V$$

**Step 3 — Check level-shift chain:**

Same as Case 1, $V_P = 0.7\,V$ is **insufficient** to forward-bias two series diodes plus the base-emitter junction (which would need $0.7 + 0.7 + 0.7 = 2.1\,V$).

**Result:** $Q_1$ OFF, $Y = V_{CC} = 5\,V$ (HIGH).

**Result:** $Y = \overline{0 \cdot 1} = \overline{0} = 1$ ✓

---

#### 3.1.4 Case 3: A = 1 (5 V), B = 1 (5 V)

**Step 1 — All input diodes reverse-biased:**

Both $D_A$ and $D_B$ have anodes at $5\,V$ and cathodes at node P. With no current path from inputs to ground, P is pulled up toward $V_{CC}$ through $R_1$.

**Step 2 — Voltage at P:**

Assuming $D_1$ and $D_2$ conduct (to be verified):

$$V_P = V_{BE(Q_1)} + V_{F(D_1)} + V_{F(D_2)} = 0.7 + 0.7 + 0.7 = 2.1\,V$$

This means $V_P$ is clamped at $2.1\,V$ (lower than $5\,V$), so the input diodes remain reverse-biased. ✓

**Step 3 — Base current calculation:**

Current through $R_1$:

$$I_{R_1} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_P}{R_1} = \frac{5 - 2.1}{5\,k\Omega} = \frac{2.9}{5000} = 0.58\,mA$$

This current splits into two paths: into the base of $Q_1$ via the level-shift diodes, and (negligibly) into the reverse-biased input diodes.

$$I_B = I_{R_1} \approx 0.58\,mA$$

**Step 4 — Check saturation condition:**

Saturation collector current:

$$I_{C(sat)} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{CE(sat)}}{R_C} = \frac{5 - 0.2}{2\,k\Omega} = 2.4\,mA$$

Required base current at edge of saturation:

$$I_{B(sat)} = \frac{I_{C(sat)}}{\beta_{FC}} = \frac{2.4\,mA}{50} = 0.048\,mA = 48\,\mu A$$

Since $I_B = 0.58\,mA \gg I_{B(sat)} = 0.048\,mA$, the transistor is **deeply saturated** (forced $\beta = 2.4/0.58 \approx 4.1$).

**Step 5 — Output Y:**

$$Y = V_{CE(sat)} = 0.2\,V \quad (\text{Logic LOW} = 0)$$

**Result:** $Y = \overline{1 \cdot 1} = \overline{1} = 0$ ✓

---

#### 3.1.5 Case 4: A = 0, B = 0 (Symmetric) → Already covered in Case 1.

**Complete Truth Table for DTL NAND:**

| A | B | $D_A$ | $D_B$ | $D_1, D_2$ | $Q_1$ State | Y |
|---|---|-------|-------|------------|-------------|---|
| 0 | 0 | ON | ON | OFF | Cut-off | 1 (HIGH) |
| 0 | 1 | ON | OFF | OFF | Cut-off | 1 (HIGH) |
| 1 | 0 | OFF | ON | OFF | Cut-off | 1 (HIGH) |
| 1 | 1 | OFF | OFF | ON | Saturation | 0 (LOW) |

$$Y = \overline{A \cdot B} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Analysis: The RTL NOR Gate

Given an RTL NOR gate with two parallel NPN transistors:
- $V_{CC} = +5\,V$, $R_C = 1\,k\Omega$
- Each base resistor $R_B = 10\,k\Omega$
- $\beta_{FC} = 50$, $V_{BE(on)} = 0.7\,V$, $V_{CE(sat)} = 0.2\,V$

**Case: A = 1 (5 V), B = 0 (0 V)**

**Step 1 — Transistor $Q_A$ analysis:**

Base current of $Q_A$:

$$I_{B(A)} = \frac{V_{in} - V_{BE}}{R_B} = \frac{5 - 0.7}{10\,k\Omega} = 0.43\,mA$$

**Step 2 — Saturation check for $Q_A$:**

$$I_{C(sat)} = \frac{5 - 0.2}{1\,k\Omega} = 4.8\,mA$$

$$I_{B(sat)} = \frac{4.8\,mA}{50} = 0.096\,mA$$

Since $I_{B(A)} = 0.43\,mA \gg 0.096\,mA$, $Q_A$ is **saturated**.

**Step 3 — Output Y:**

$$Y = V_{CE(sat)} = 0.2\,V \quad (\text{Logic LOW} = 0)$$

This matches $Y = \overline{1 + 0} = \overline{1} = 0$ ✓

**Case: A = 0, B = 0**

Both bases have $V_{in} = 0\,V < 0.7\,V$ → both transistors in cut-off → $Y = V_{CC} = 5\,V$ (HIGH).

Result: $Y = \overline{0 + 0} = \overline{0} = 1$ ✓

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Threshold Voltages for Diode Activation

| Gate | Diode Configuration | Conducting Condition | Output Logic |
|------|---------------------|---------------------|--------------|
| **Diode AND** | Anodes at inputs, cathodes at output | Any input LOW $\implies$ output LOW | $Y = A \cdot B$ |
| **Diode OR** | Cathodes at inputs, anodes at output | Any input HIGH $\implies$ output HIGH | $Y = A + B$ |
| **DTL NAND** | AND stage + level-shift + inverter | All inputs HIGH $\implies$ transistor ON | $Y = \overline{A \cdot B}$ |
| **RTL NOR** | Parallel transistor switches | Any input HIGH $\implies$ output LOW | $Y = \overline{A + B}$ |
| **NOT Inverter** | Single BJT common-emitter | Input HIGH $\implies$ output LOW | $Y = \overline{A}$ |
